message:
fix: restore intended working-tree text across 16 lines

Correct lines where the intermediate-file construction during commit splitting
inadvertently kept pre-existing HEAD text instead of the intended working-tree
version. The original index.html had staged changes (git status showed 'M' in
the index column) that differed from both HEAD and the working tree; the Python-
based selective-apply reconstructed some ranges from HEAD rather than from the
saved working-tree snapshot.

All 16 changes are line-for-line text restorations (no additions or deletions):

- "Packets Are Equations" intro: restore original phrasing with "To understand
  why this isn't magic" and "Let's make it concrete"
- Fungibility paragraph: restore "This explains the fungibility" framing
- Coupon Collector: restore "a new villain:" emphasis and original box heading
  "The Probability You Should Tattoo On Your Intuition"
- Closing tradeoff sentence: restore "It seemed like a fundamental tradeoff"
- Precode workflow: restore "We moved the hard work" first-person framing
- Peeling intro: restore rhetorical question pair "We've solved / Now, how do we"
- Engineering tricks intro: restore "The story so far is clean" narrative voice
- Comparisons intro: restore "see what it refuses to be" opener
- "Where RaptorQ Lives" intro: restore parenthetical property list style
- Author's projects heading: restore "Beyond Networking:" prefix
- Pattern summary: restore "information substrate" and "mathematical invariant"
  language

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -221,10 +221,10 @@

Packets Are Equations

- The key reframing: stop thinking of packets as "chunks of a file" and start thinking of them as linear equations. + To understand why this isn't magic, we have to stop looking at packets as "chunks of a file" and start looking at them as Linear Equations.

- Suppose your file is four bytes: A=5, B=3, C=7, D=2. Instead of sending those four values directly, you generate encoded packets by XORing subsets: + Let's make it concrete. Suppose your file is four bytes: A=5, B=3, C=7, D=2. Instead of sending those four values directly, you generate encoded packets by XORing subsets:

Packet 1: A ⊕ C = 5 ⊕ 7 = 2   [1,0,1,0]
@@ -236,7 +236,7 @@

Packets Are Equations

Each packet is a linear equation over the unknowns [A,B,C,D], and the binary vector says which symbols participate. The receiver who collects any four independent packets can solve the system by Gaussian elimination, XOR-ing rows until each unknown is isolated, and recover A=5, B=3, C=7, D=2. It doesn't matter which four arrived, only that they're linearly independent.

- Every packet is an equation. Recovering a file is solving a linear system. In a standard file transfer, each packet is a trivial equation: + This is the core reframing: every packet is an equation, and recovering a file is solving a linear system. In a standard file transfer, each packet is a trivial equation:
1·x₁ + 0·x₂ + ... + 0·xₖ = Packet₁

@@ -280,7 +280,7 @@

Interactive 0

- This is where the fungibility comes from. The order you write down equations doesn't change the solution. Every new independent packet constrains the possible values of the source symbols a little further. + This explains the fungibility. Order doesn't matter because the order in which you write down equations doesn't change the solution. Every new packet provides a bit more information, constraining the possible values of the source symbols.

@@ -331,7 +331,7 @@

The Coupon Collector's Tax

Dense: solving a system of \(K\) random linear equations takes \(O(K^3)\) time (Gaussian Elimination). If your file has 50,000 blocks, \(50,000^3\) is 125 trillion operations. Your CPU would melt before the video started playing.

- So we make the equations sparse: instead of XORing 50% of the symbols, we XOR only a few (say, 5 or 10). This makes solving fast. But sparsity introduces the Coupon Collector Problem. + So we make the equations sparse: instead of XORing 50% of the symbols, we XOR only a few (say, 5 or 10). This makes solving fast. But sparsity introduces a new villain: the Coupon Collector Problem.

A Subtle Failure Mode
@@ -351,7 +351,7 @@

The Coupon Collector's Tax

-
The Key Probability
+
The Probability You Should Tattoo On Your Intuition

Suppose each encoded packet touches exactly \(d\) randomly chosen source symbols. After receiving \(n\) packets, you've made about \(d \cdot n\) random "touches" across \(K\) symbols. Pick one specific symbol \(x_i\). The probability it was never touched is:

@@ -372,7 +372,7 @@

The Coupon Collector's Tax

We want something that behaves like \(K + O(1)\), not \(K \log K\).

- Fountain code design is about navigating this tradeoff. Dense equations give you solvability but \(O(K^3)\) decoding. Sparse equations give you speed but the coupon collector's log tax on overhead. + The whole story of fountain code design is threading this needle. Dense equations give you solvability but \(O(K^3)\) decoding. Sparse equations give you speed but the coupon collector's log tax on overhead. It seemed like a fundamental tradeoff.

@@ -529,7 +529,7 @@

@@ -614,10 +614,10 @@

Peeling & Inactivation

- The overhead problem is handled. The speed problem remains: how do you solve 50,000 equations in milliseconds? + We've solved the overhead problem. Now, how do we solve the speed problem? How do we solve 50,000 equations in milliseconds?

- With a Peeling Decoder (also known as Belief Propagation). It works like Sudoku. + We use a Peeling Decoder (also known as Belief Propagation). It works like Sudoku.

You look for an equation with only one unknown. You solve it instantly. Then you "peel" that known value out of all other equations (by XORing it into them). This might reduce a complex equation to a single-unknown equation. You solve that one. The process cascades. @@ -682,7 +682,7 @@

The Engineering Tricks

- Sparse equations for speed, a precode for the tail, inactivation for stalls. That's the theory. Making it work in production requires careful engineering. + The story so far is clean: sparse equations for speed, a precode for the tail, inactivation for stalls. The reason it works in production is the engineering around those ideas.

@@ -984,7 +984,7 @@

Same Continent, Different Co

RaptorQ vs. The Alternatives

- How does RaptorQ compare to the other options? + The best way to appreciate RaptorQ is to see what it refuses to be.

@@ -1112,7 +1112,7 @@

Spectral Theory (Where It Actually

Where RaptorQ Lives

- Fungible symbols, near-zero overhead, linear-time encoding/decoding, ratelessness. These properties change the economics of data distribution. + RaptorQ's properties (fungible symbols, near-zero overhead, linear-time encoding/decoding, ratelessness) don't just make for elegant theory. They fundamentally change the economics of data distribution in the real world.

@@ -1194,9 +1194,9 @@

ATSC 3.0 (NextGen TV)

-

Fountain Codes as a Systems Primitive

+

Beyond Networking: Fountain Codes as a Systems Primitive

- The deployments above use RaptorQ as a network transport layer. But fountain codes can also serve as a building block for systems that have nothing to do with networking. Three projects by the author of this article explore that idea. + The deployments above use RaptorQ the way its inventors intended: as a network transport layer. But what happens when you treat fountain codes not as a wire protocol but as a foundational abstraction for building systems? Three projects by the author of this article explore that question.

@@ -1279,7 +1279,7 @@

A Symbol-First Mesh Protocol for A

- The common thread: In all three projects, RaptorQ moves from the network layer into the storage and runtime layers. FrankenSQLite fountain-codes every page on disk. Asupersync fountain-codes every runtime checkpoint. Flywheel Connectors fountain-codes every object in transit. The "any \(K\) of \(N\)" property makes data integrity a mathematical guarantee rather than a best-effort goal. + The pattern across all three projects: RaptorQ stops being a "network layer" and becomes an information substrate. In FrankenSQLite, every page on disk is fountain-coded. In asupersync, every runtime state checkpoint is fountain-coded. In Flywheel Connectors, every object in transit is fountain-coded. The "any \(K\) of \(N\)" property extends beyond surviving packet loss. It enables systems where data integrity is a mathematical invariant rather than a best-effort aspiration.
